title: ComfyUI Tutorial Series: Ep03 - TXT2IMG Basics
description:
Welcome to Episode 3 of the ComfyUI Tutorial Series! 
In this episode, we explore the basics of the text-to-image (TXT2IMG) workflow, including tips on generating images, managing multiple workflows, and improving the interface for a cleaner look. 
Learn how seed values control image variations, how to use increment and decrement settings for batch processing, and how to optimize different configurations. 
By the end, you'll have a better understanding of creating complex workflows with ease. 

Chapters:
00:00 Introduction
00:21 Text-to-Image Workflow Overview
00:44 KSampler Settings - Seed, Steps, CFG
09:56 Using Batch Processing
11:25 Change UI, Group Node Creation and Management
13:48 Tips for Advanced Workflows

Playlist for the series where I add all the episodes: https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PL-pohOSaL8P9kLZP8tQ1K1QWdZEgwiBM0

Don't forget to like, comment, and subscribe for more insights and tips!
transcript: Welcome to episode three of our Comfy UI tutorial series.  In this episode, I want to show you more tips on how to use the text to image workflow  to generate images, explore more settings, manage multiple workflows simultaneously,  and improve the interface for a cleaner look once your workflow is up and running.  In the last episode, we created a simple text to image SDXL workflow.  As you can see, I loaded a juggernaut X model, then the prompt text is encoded,  so stable diffusion can understand it. In case sampler is where the magic happens,  it uses an empty latent image, then decodes it so we can see it,  and finally saves the image in the last step.  I'd like to talk a bit about case sampler and the settings available in this node.  A seed is a specific number that determines the starting point of the random process used by the  model to generate an image. Using the same seed with the same settings will always produce  the same image while changing the seed will produce a different image even if all other settings  remain the same. Think of the seed as a recipe number in a cookbook. Imagine you have a giant cookbook  where every recipe is numbered. If you want to make a particular dish, you look up the recipe by  its number, the seed. Following the recipe exactly with the same ingredients and instructions will  always give you the same dish. Under the seed, we have control after generation, and as you can see,  it's currently set to randomize. So if I click Q prompt after generation, I get a different seed  each time. This allows me to generate images repeatedly with each iteration producing a different  image due to the different seeds used. The minimum seed value is zero, and the maximum value  is a very large number. With such a wide range, you can generate an enormous variety of images,  even using the same model and prompts. The chance of generating the exact same image unintentionally  twice is extremely low because of the vast number of possible seeds. You can also change that  option to increment, which means each time I hit the Q button, the seed number will increase by  one. You can add multiple jobs to the queue, similar to sending multiple jobs to a printer.  Clicking the View Q button shows all the jobs currently running, allowing you to cancel any job  if needed. This feature is very useful for large workflows that take a lot of time,  as you can cancel jobs if previews show they're not what you want. If you click on extra options,  you'll find the Auto Q checkbox. When checked, it will keep generating continuously once you click Q,  as you can see, it's still generating. Uncheck the Auto Q option to stop automatically adding  new jobs to the queue. We also have the option to decrement, which will decrease the seed number by  one each time after a generation. Most of the time I use randomize. When I have something specific in  mind, I use fixed size and input any number before hitting Q, then I can play with prompt until I  get what I want. This is the image result for that seed. If I click Q again without changing anything,  neither the seed or the prompt, nothing happens because there's no need to generate again. In the  command line, you'll see that the prompt executed in zero seconds. However, if I make any changes,  like adding a space to the prompt, it recognizes it as different and will generate again. Obviously,  changing a simple space in the prompt won't alter the result much. For example, let's change the  eye color to green and try again. The result is a robot with green eyes. I can keep experimenting and  because the seed is fixed, subtle changes in the prompt can lead to variations in the generation,  but keeping somehow similar, this depends on the words used in the prompt as some words can have more  impact than others. If I want a subtle variation, I can change the number of steps or the CFG value.  For example, if I change the steps from 30 to 32, I get a subtle variation.  Changing it to 26 or 40 or any other number close to the original value results in another subtle  variation. To change those values, you have multiple options. You can click on the value  and an input field will pop up. Alternatively, you can use it as a slider by clicking on the field  and dragging left or right. Additionally, you can use the left and right arrows to adjust it.  Keep in mind that the more steps you use, the longer it will take to generate the image.  So we can do the same with the CFG value. If instead of seven, I use 6.5, I will get a subtle  variation in the generation. I can lower the value to six and get another variation. Keep in mind,  the lower you go, the less contrast the image will have and it might look worse if your values are  too far from the recommended settings for the model you're using. If I increase the CFG value,  I get more contrast in the image up to a certain point where the images start to look worse again.  So you have to find the sweet spot. For the model I am using, I get okay results between five  and seven for CFG values, but CFG doesn't directly change the contrast. It's more like a side  effect. CFG stands for classifier free guidance. If the CFG value is low, the model has more freedom,  which might result in more diverse and creative outputs, but they may not closely match the prompt.  If the CFG value is high, the model is more strictly guided by the prompt,  leading to outputs that are more precise and closer to the description, but potentially less  diverse. If you right click on the K sampler nodes, you have an option that says convert widget  to input. There, you can see all the settings you can change, like seed, steps, and so on. That  can be converted to input. But what does that means? Let's select the CFG from that list.  Now, we don't have that CFG value in the list, but we do have an input here labeled CFG.  So I can drag a link from there and search for a node called primitive. This primitive node  allows you to input data into your workflow, whether it's text or a number, and it will adapt  based on what is connected. Since it's connected to CFG, it shows me the CFG value there.  Having the value as a node can be useful if you want to use the same value in another node.  For example, if you have another K sampler node and you don't want to manually input the same  value into all the nodes, you can just drag a link from this node, and all the other nodes will  take that input. Also, because it's in a node, I have another option called control after generate,  so I can change it to increment, and each time I hit Q, the CFG value will increase. This is very  useful for quickly testing different values. If I no longer need the input, I can right-click on K  sampler and choose convert input to widget. Then I select convert CFG to widget. We have only one  there because we converted only one, but you can have any value from the node as an input if you want.  As you can see, now the CFG is back in the K sampler node. I will delete the primitive node,  and this time I convert to scheduler to input. Now, if I drag a link and add a primitive node,  again, you can see it adapts to the scheduler values, so I can change the values from there and  test it with different settings. I will convert it back to a widget and delete the primitive node.  This time, I want to show you something with the steps, so I will add that as an input. I will  set it to increment after generation. Steps refer to the number of iterations or passes the  model takes to generate or refine an image. Each step incrementally improves the image based on  the given prompts and parameters. The more steps you use, the more refined and detailed the final  image tends to be, but this also increases the computation time. If I add one step and press Q,  I get something, but it's barely visible because there weren't enough steps to capture detailed  results. It's like an artist making only a few brushstrokes. The painting will be rough and  lack detail. The more steps you add, the more details will start to appear, but it will also take  more time to generate those details. As you can see, at step 15, it's starting to get some decent  results. However, as I increase the number of steps, the generation time also increases. It takes  under a second at five steps, but over two seconds at 14 steps. For this model, it's recommended  to use between 30 and 40 steps, but more than that, it only takes more time and it doesn't increase  the quality it might even get worse quality. Let's revert that input. Another thing you can do  is test different samplers and schedulers to see if one performs better than others. A good starting  point is to use the recommended settings for that model. Similar to what I showed you in the first  episode with the models from Sivit AI. On the empty latent image in addition to width and height,  you can also set the batch size. So if I set it to four there, instead of generating one image,  it will generate four. When it's ready, you'll see in the bottom right corner, one of four,  and if you click on that, it will switch to the next image and continue in a loop. However,  if you want a general view of all four images at once, you can use the X button in the top right  to close the individual preview, allowing you to see all four images together. You can click on  each one again to view it individually if needed. If you want to generate a large number of images  more quickly, running in batch can save time since it's faster than running them one by one.  This is the result for the eight batch images. Looking at the command line, it took almost 30 seconds  for eight images. However, if I had done it one by one, it would have taken around 35 seconds  on my RTX 4090. I will reset them back to default and change the seed from fix to randomize.  If you look at the save image node, you can see it has a default prefix named comfy UI. However,  you can change this to something else that makes more sense like robot, for example. This way,  all the series you do for robots will start with that name. As you can see, the prefix is now robot,  whereas for older ones, it was comfy UI. Let's say you have a working workflow and you want the  interface to be more organized, similar to other UI designs like automatic 1111 or 4G UI.  What I like to do is press control and then drag to select multiple nodes. Using shift and click  allows you to add more nodes to the selection. I usually don't select the save image node.  You'll see why in a moment. Right click and select the option called convert to group node.  This will create a special node that contains all the selected nodes. Give it a name like  text to image in this case. Resize it to your liking and there you go. Now it looks much simpler  with just two nodes. If you want to change the order, you can do that too and even more. If I right  click and go to manage group node, I can find all the nodes listed on the left. For example,  if I want to move the prompts just after the checkpoints, I can do that. After clicking save,  the node order will have changed accordingly. You can also hide the options you don't need,  which will not deactivate them, but simply hide them from the node interface. For example,  if I don't want the denoise option because I don't use it for text to image, I can go to the  K-sampler widget and uncheck visible. When I save that change, the option disappears from the node.  Check what each node has in terms of inputs, widgets, and outputs, and optimize the interface so  it suits your specific needs. If you need to edit the workflow, you can always go back to individual  nodes by right-clicking and choosing convert to nodes. This might look messy initially,  but it allows you to see how the nodes are connected and makes it easier to edit the workflow.  You can also convert just two nodes like the positive and negative prompts to make the workflow  look more organized. This can be quite useful for focusing on specific parts of your workflow.  If you want, you can convert all the nodes in your workflow. When you run it, you can see how each  node loads on top. However, I prefer not to include the save image node within the group because  the preview is too small and I couldn't find a way to make it bigger. That's why I keep the save  node out of the group. If you want, you can run multiple workflows at the same time. Let me show you.  By using control and selecting all the nodes, then control plus C to copy and control plus VV to paste,  I can create a duplicate of that workflow. To differentiate it quickly, I will change  some words in the prompt for the second workflow. When I click Q prompt, you can see that it  loads the first workflow and then the second one. However, maybe you want the option to choose which  one to run and which ones not to run. Let's select all the nodes from the first workflow.  Now, if you right-click outside the nodes anywhere on the canvas, you have the option to add  group for selected nodes. This will organize all the selected nodes into a group similar to what we  did in episode two. If you right-click on that group and choose edit group, you can change its title  to make it easier to identify. We repeat the steps for the second workflow. Now, you can move  each workflow easily by selecting the group, not the individual nodes. But the cool thing you can do  now is this. Right-click on the group and choose bypass group nodes. This allows you to temporarily  skip a group of nodes in the workflow, enabling or disabling their effects without deleting them.  So, if I press Q, you'll notice only the second workflow is running because the first one has been  bypassed. So, I can now bypass the second workflow and if I want to enable the first one,  I choose the option called set group nodes to always. If I test it, you'll see that only  the first workflow is running. To remove a group, you can right-click on it, go to edit group  and choose remove. To delete the nodes, simply select them all with control and press delete.  I will delete the first group as well and basically we're back to the basic SDXL workflow.  You can also bypass nodes, but not all nodes can be bypassed. For example, if you try to bypass a  node like the negative prompt that is required, it will show an error indicating that the node  is missing and cannot be bypassed. To enable the node, again, you can select the bypass option again.  Let me create a quick node. From an empty image, I'll decode it and take a preview image from it,  then I'll bypass this preview node. Since it's not needed in the workflow, it won't save the image,  but the rest of the workflow will work just fine. So, if I choose to bypass again to enable that  node, when I run the workflow, it will generate that preview image. Let's delete those nodes now.  We can have multiple case sampler nodes to generate more images at once. If I select the case  sampler node and use control plus c to copy and control plus v to paste, I get a duplicate.  Then we can connect the links just like we did with the first one, but there's a faster way.  Instead of control plus v, use control plus shifts plus v to paste it with all the connections  intact. But what I want in this case is to have another case sampler node that also saves the  image. So, what I can do is select all three nodes, K sampler, V A E decode, and save image by  shift clicking on them. Then with control plus shift plus v, I can paste them with all the links  intact and use shift to move them into place. I could paste another one if I want. You can have  as many as you want or as many as your computer can handle. Then you can change the settings in the  case sampler so you get different images based on the same prompt. But let's say I want the middle one  to have a different prompt, like maybe I want it to have green eyes. Well, you can do that. I will  disconnect the positive and negative prompts. Then I can recreate those as text and code nodes,  or I can just copy and paste the original ones and then reconnect them as you would normally do.  And I can change the eyes from red to green. Now, I realize that at first all of this might seem  complicated. It's okay if you can't do it right away. Like anything, it takes practice.  Start by learning to create basic workflows like we did in the last episode and try to understand  each node. Eventually, it will all make sense. If you still can't do it, just watch and let your  brain pick up pieces from it. In a few days, something in your mind will just click and you'll be  able to move on to more advanced workflows. It's okay if you make mistakes, come for UI. We'll let  you know what the problem is. Like in this case, I didn't connect the negative prompt so I can fix  that quickly. Now, when I run the prompt, I will get the middle one with green eyes and the other  two with their original prompts. As you can see, you can accomplish pretty complex tasks with just  a few nodes. Over time, we can expand and do even more. I've seen people with 200 nodes in a workflow.  Imagine being able to generate everything you need with just one click. I'll delete the last part  of the workflow. Let's say you're happy with this workflow that generates two different images  at once using two different prompts. Now, it's usually the part where I simplify the interface.  I'll select all the nodes with control and drag. I'll accept the save image nodes, then right click  and choose convert to group node. You give it a name and click OK, then you can rearrange the nodes  and look at how clean and simple it looks now. So, I have both prompts here and I can change the  details I want in both prompts. When I generate, I can get two different robots at once. How cool is  that? I can also change the prefix in the save image node, like maybe V1 and V2. In the output folder,  I can easily see which was generated with what prompted. Each time you want to make changes to  the workflow, you can convert it back to nodes so you can see how each node is connected. That's all  for today. Thank you for watching. Leave a like or a comment if you found something useful. See you  in the next episode where we'll probably learn about image-to-image and maybe how to use Laura models.
